content.text: Two operators introduced in modern JavaScript — optional chaining (

) — solve two of the most common problems when working with data that might be missing: accessing nested properties safely, and providing fallback values only when something is truly absent. Both are used constantly in React when handling API data, optional props, and uninitialized state.
When you access a property on
. Optional chaining short-circuits the access and returns
operator provides a fallback value, but only when the left side is
. This is its key difference from

// || falls back for ALL falsy values
0 || 100          // 100  — wrong when 0 is a valid value!
'' || 'default'   // 'default' — wrong when empty string is valid!
false || true     // true  — may not be what you want

// ?? only falls back for null and undefined
0 ?? 100          // 0     — keeps the valid zero
'' ?? 'default'   // ''    — keeps the valid empty string
false ?? true     // false — keeps the valid false
null ?? 'default' // 'default' — correct fallback
undefined ?? 'default' // 'default' — correct fallback
The two operators compose naturally: optional chaining safely accesses a potentially missing value, and nullish coalescing provides the fallback when that value is missing:
Rendering data from a loading state
Safely accessing nested API response data
assigns only if the variable is currently
Using || instead of ?? for numeric/boolean defaults
Overusing ?. when the object is guaranteed to exist
Forgetting ?. only checks null/undefined — not other falsy values
(optional chaining) safely accesses a property or calls a method on
(nullish coalescing) provides a fallback only when the left side is
— safely traverses nested paths and falls back gracefully.
provides meaningful defaults for missing or optional props and data.
